summary: Chinese-American science fiction author. Most commonly identified by 'Story of Your Life' (linguist Louise Banks, Heptapod B, Fermat's principle of least time, adapted into the film Arrival), 'Tower of Babylon' (Hillalum, miner from Elam), and 'Exhalation' (air-powered being dissects own brain). Other works include 'What's Expected of Us' (the Predictor), 'Hell is the Absence of God,' and 'Liking What You See: A Documentary' (calliagnosia).

works.name: Story of Your Life
works.description: Chiang's best-known work, adapted into the film Arrival. Linguist Dr. Louise Banks nicknames two heptapod aliens Flapper and Raspberry and works to learn the language Heptapod B. The story explores the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis and Fermat's principle of least time. It begins and ends with Banks telling her daughter about her husband asking 'Do you want to make a baby?' By learning Heptapod B, Banks is able to perceive her entire life at once.

clues.clue: Linguist Louise Banks deciphers Heptapod B; nicknames aliens Flapper and Raspberry
examples: Louise Banks works with the aliens Flapper and Raspberry to learn the language Heptapod B
a linguist who is tasked with the job of deciphering the language of the Heptapods

clues.clue: Uses Fermat's principle of least time; includes diagrams of air-water boundary

this sci-fi author whose 'Story of Your Life' is the basis for the movie Arrival

examples: a story which both begins and ends with the narrator telling her daughter about her husband asking her 'Do you want to make a baby?'

works.name: Tower of Babylon
works.description: Hillalum, a miner from Elam, travels with a caravan up the title structure. He tunnels through what he thinks is the vault of heaven but finds himself in Shinar. The universe is revealed to be shaped like a seal cylinder, and the tower loops back to the surface.

clues.clue: Hillalum, miner from Elam, climbs the title structure; universe shaped like a seal cylinder; loops back to surface
examples: Hillalum travels with a caravan up the title structure and learns that the universe is shaped like a seal cylinder

clues.clue: Narrator shocked to find himself in Shinar after tunneling through vault of heaven
examples: The narrator of a story by this author is shocked to find himself in Shinar after tunneling through what he thought was the vault of heaven

works.name: What's Expected of Us
works.description: A story about the Predictor, a device that always flashes a light one second before you press a button, proving free will is an illusion. This causes a 'legion of Bartleby the Scriveners' who no longer act spontaneously. Ends with the narrator saying 'free will is an illusion... Because I had no choice.'

clues.clue: The Predictor flashes a light a second before you press a button; proves free will is an illusion
examples: a device called the 'Predictor' that always flashes a light a second before you press a button
the Predictor, a button that flashes a second before you press it

clues.clue: 'A legion of Bartleby the Scriveners' who no longer act spontaneously
examples: describes 'a legion of Bartleby the Scriveners' who no longer act spontaneously

works.name: Exhalation (title story)
works.description: In the title story of Chiang's collection, an air-powered being dissects its own brain and discovers flowing currents of argon. Scientists theorize that memories are engraved on sheets of gold foil in their skulls.

clues.clue: Air-powered being dissects its own brain; discovers flowing currents of argon; memories on gold foil
examples: scientists theorize that all of a robot's memories are engraved on sheets of gold foil in their skulls, leading one to operate on himself and discover flowing currents of argon power him
An air-powered being dissects its own brain in the title story of this author's collection Exhalation

works.name: Hell is the Absence of God
works.description: A story in which Janice Reilly is given legs during an angelic visit and goes on a pilgrimage with the minister Ethan Mead. A character dying in a car crash is struck by a beam of light from Heaven inspiring him to love God, but God sends him to Hell anyway. Ken Liu's 'Single-Bit Error' was written in response.

clues.clue: Character dying in car crash struck by beam of light from Heaven; loves God but sent to Hell anyway
examples: one of this author's characters is struck by a beam of light from Heaven which inspires him to love God with all his being, but God sends him to Hell anyway

clues.clue: Janice Reilly given legs during angelic visit; pilgrimage with minister Ethan Mead
examples: Janice Reilly, who is given legs during an angelic visit, goes on a pilgrimage with the minister Ethan Mead

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Additional works mentioned include 'Liking What You See: A Documentary' (Pembleton College implements calliagnosia to eliminate lookism), 'The Merchant and the Alchemist's Gate' (Baghdad narrator, inspired by Kip Thorne, opens the Exhalation collection), 'Anxiety is the Dizziness of Freedom' (titled from Kierkegaard quote, prism allowing communication with alternate selves). Chiang is identified as Chinese-American.

clues.clue: 'Liking What You See: A Documentary': calliagnosia prevents people from perceiving attractiveness; Pembleton College votes on neuro-implant
examples: Pembleton College implements a technology called calliagnosia, which prevents people from being able to tell whether someone is good-looking or not

clues.clue: 'The Merchant and the Alchemist's Gate': Baghdad narrator, time travel, inspired by Kip Thorne; opens Exhalation
examples: The story 'The Merchant and the Alchemist's Gate' opens this man's most recent collection, Exhalation

clues.clue: 'Anxiety is the Dizziness of Freedom': Kierkegaard quote title; prism for communicating with alternate selves
examples: This author used a Kierkegaard quote to title his story 'Anxiety is the Dizziness of Freedom'

comprehensive_summary:
Ted Chiang is a Chinese-American science fiction author whose works appear primarily at higher difficulty levels in quizbowl. His most commonly clued story by far is 'Story of Your Life,' the basis for the film Arrival. In it, linguist Dr. Louise Banks nicknames two heptapod aliens Flapper and Raspberry and works to learn the language Heptapod B, exploring the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis and Fermat's principle of least time. The story begins and ends with Banks telling her daughter about her husband's question 'Do you want to make a baby?' and by learning Heptapod B, she perceives her entire life simultaneously.

'Tower of Babylon' is the second most frequently clued work, featuring Hillalum, a miner from Elam, who climbs the title structure with a caravan. After tunneling through what he thinks is the vault of heaven, he finds himself back in Shinar, as the universe is shaped like a seal cylinder. 'What's Expected of Us' introduces the Predictor, a device proving free will is an illusion by flashing a second before you press its button, creating 'a legion of Bartleby the Scriveners.'

The title story of the collection Exhalation features an air-powered being who dissects its own brain and discovers flowing currents of argon, with memories engraved on gold foil. 'Hell is the Absence of God' features a character who loves God after being struck by heavenly light during a car crash but is sent to Hell anyway. 'Liking What You See: A Documentary' depicts Pembleton College voting on calliagnosia technology to eliminate lookism. 'Anxiety is the Dizziness of Freedom,' titled from a Kierkegaard quote, involves a prism for communicating with alternate selves.
